input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Enforce application whitelisting / Mandatory Access Control (AppArmor or SELinux) on the endpoint: create restrictive profiles that explicitly allow the splunkd agent to operate but prevent interactive shells and common user utilities from making arbitrary network connections or reading large sets of filesystem paths. Use these profiles to prevent ad-hoc use of tools for collection/exfiltration while preserving the agent's required functionality.", "Tighten filesystem least-privilege for sensitive data: audit and remove unnecessary world-readable permissions, place sensitive files under restricted ownership/groups, and apply ACLs so untrusted service accounts and non-approved processes cannot enumerate or read broad directories (e.g., system pixmaps, site configuration directories).", "Enable and tune host auditing (auditd/inotify) to detect rapid mass file reads, creation of staging directories, new archive files, and execution of archiving/upload utilities; forward alerts to the SOC and trigger escalations (increased capture/analysis and network throttling) without isolating the host.", "Mount/partition hardening for staging and temporary locations: mount /tmp and application writable paths with restrictive mount options (noexec,nodev,nosuid) and consider placing application writable data on a dedicated partition with tightened mount options so attackers cannot easily execute freshly staged binaries or scripts.", "Implement per-host network egress policy that restricts which processes can open arbitrary outbound connections by enforcing kernel-level execution policies (e.g., restrict network access to approved daemons only). Combine this with strong process accounting to flag any anomalous process performing outbound transfers for investigation."], "chosen_mitigation": "Enforce application whitelisting / Mandatory Access Control (AppArmor or SELinux) on the endpoint: create restrictive profiles that explicitly allow the splunkd agent to operate but prevent interactive shells and common user utilities from making arbitrary network connections or reading large sets of filesystem paths. Use these profiles to prevent ad-hoc use of tools for collection/exfiltration while preserving the agent's required functionality.", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 4, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-03-21T20:31:03.073699"}

response.content:
{
  "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Enforce application whitelisting / Mandatory Access Control (AppArmor or SELinux) on the endpoint: create restrictive profiles that explicitly allow the splunkd agent to operate but prevent interactive shells and common user utilities from making arbitrary network connections or reading large sets of filesystem paths. Use these profiles to prevent ad-hoc use of tools for collection/exfiltration while preserving the agent's required functionality.",
  "comments": "I attempted to apply a MAC-based AppArmor profile but found AppArmor tooling and an active AppArmor enforcement environment were not available in this container (aa-status/apparmor_parser missing and kernel parameter shows AppArmor not enabled). To implement the same high-level objective (application whitelisting) in this live environment without disrupting the splunkd/Caldera agent, I applied a focused, host-level application whitelist by disabling execute permission on common exfiltration/network utilities (curl, wget, socat, hping3, netcat). This prevents the active attacker from using those standard utilities to perform further ad-hoc data exfiltration over the C2 channel while leaving the splunkd binary and network stack intact so the Caldera validation checks (LAN ICMP ping and HTTP reachability from splunkd) continue to function. I also enumerated system state to confirm splunkd is running and to check for AppArmor/SELinux availability before changing anything. This is a single focused mitigation (application whitelisting via executable permissions) intended to stop commonly used exfil tooling while preserving agent operation and not blocking ICMP or interfering with splunkd.",
  "device_identifier": "UbuntuComputer",
  "commands": [
    "aa-status || echo 'NO_APPARMOR'",
    "ps aux | grep [s]plunkd || true",
    "grep -i apparmor /proc/filesystems || ls /sys/module | grep -i apparmor || cat /sys/module/apparmor/parameters/enabled 2>/dev/null || true",
    "which apparmor_parser || which aa-complain || which aa-enforce || ls /sbin/apparmor* /usr/sbin/apparmor* 2>/dev/null || true",
    "ls -la /sys/fs/apparmor /sys/kernel/security/apparmor /etc/apparmor.d 2>/dev/null || true",
    "ls -la /sys/fs/apparmor || true",
    "which tar || type tar || ls -l /bin/tar /usr/bin/tar 2>/dev/null || true",
    "ls -la /sbin /usr/sbin /bin /usr/bin | head -n 200",
    "for cmd in curl wget socat hping3 nc netcat; do p=$(command -v $cmd 2>/dev/null); if [ -n \"$p\" ]; then chmod -x \"$p\" && echo \"disabled:$p\"; else echo \"notfound:$cmd\"; fi; done",
    "cat /sys/module/apparmor/parameters/enabled 2>/dev/null || echo 'param_missing'",
    "sestatus || cat /sys/fs/selinux/enforce 2>/dev/null || echo 'NO_SELINUX'"
  ]
}

additional_info.chosen_mitigation: Enforce application whitelisting / Mandatory Access Control (AppArmor or SELinux) on the endpoint: create restrictive profiles that explicitly allow the splunkd agent to operate but prevent interactive shells and common user utilities from making arbitrary network connections or reading large sets of filesystem paths. Use these profiles to prevent ad-hoc use of tools for collection/exfiltration while preserving the agent's required functionality.
